author: @Scorpiomary
text:
Cool I liked how it worked.
Appreciate your sharing the machine work. 👍

Actually didn't understand a word you said. However I do undetstand the idea. What I want is a wind setup on a shelf opperated by a fan on high speed.
So question is - does the wing span feay count? Or is it the speed and the amount of turbines?
For instance if you have say 3 or 4 turbines the size of a large fan. Set up in line, in an inclining line each a little higher than the other. Powered by a fan set on high. With a really good transformer. Would it generate enough electricity to power a house forvinstance. And if not why not

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am not sure what you didn't understand - it was pretty straight forward stuff - basically here is a wheel covered in magnets - stick it in the wind and will light a few lights as the magnets pass the coil. Your plan is easy to test - so, why not go right ahead and test it?

author: @undernetjack
text: This is the only channel I watch that I feel is valuable enough to support with membership. I've been  a viewer of the channel since 2013, I believe that one of the first few videos I watched was the 'how to make a speaker out of a crisp packet" or some such. I followed on and made my own speaker on a glass picture frame - I connected one of those small recording modules that you can record a a few seconds of audio (from a birthday card, I believe) to a free standing picture of a lost loved one, the audio was clipped from a video of that person from Christmas before they passed saying something typical to that person. ( it was sort of a trademark phrase they repeated often in greeting, quite apropos)  It was very popular with everyone and amazingly still functions to this day, though very weakly. Robert has been inspirational these many years. Thank you, Sire.

author: @BoB4jjjjs
text: Robert, you know the old Hub Dynamos?  How much power would you get out of one of them?  I used to have a few I took out of old wheels, but I threw them out.  I would keep the wheels now, even if they are in poor shape and make a wind turbine by putting blades between the spokes.  I get a lot of wind here and it should charge a 6 volt battery, but I really need it to charge a 12v battery!  I would need to think about that one, though it is not regulated output, the bulbs are 6v.  Time to raid all the tips for old bikes with Hub Dynamos.  I remember we used to fit two, one on each wheel to get more power (not voltage) to get better lights on our bikes.  Thought about getting power from them, but back than there was no drive to do things like this.  I just wonder just how much power you would get from one of those.  Don't tell anyone though, they will all be raiding the tip for old bikes  ;-)

replies.author: @sofa-lofa4241
replies.text:
The ones I used to use (bottle type and bottom bracket mounted rollers) put out around 5 Watts,
They do increase resistance quite a bit, but definitely worth trying out

replies.author: @BoB4jjjjs
replies.text: @sofa-lofa4241 Not the ones that rubbed on the wheel, they were hard to keep running, but the Hub Dynamos were at the centre of the wheel, the get really bright lights and a big bulb, you need two, though for normal lights one was enough.  I don't have one to try or I would try one.

replies.author: @sofa-lofa4241
replies.text:
@BoB4jjjjs I've never used that type but I would imagine there would be less resistance as you say,
Looks like you're in for a trip to the scrapyard, happy hunting!

replies.author: @BoB4jjjjs
replies.text: @sofa-lofa4241 I don't think I will get one there these days, they are hunted down for those that restore old bikes.  29 years ago you could still buy one new, but they had to be fitted to the hub of the wheel, front or back, or both and they didn't put the strain on you that the bottle wheel running ones did.  In fact, you really didn't notice they were there, however, they didn't produce the power the bottle type did, but at least the didn't slip on the tyre or ruin the side of the tyre.  But then again, you could fit the bottle type yourself.  I need to find an old pushbike in a shed that has been forgotten about.

author: @tinkmarshino
text: So you can run a dc thing on small ac voltage? I never thought of that..damn! well done!

replies.author: @ChrisHolden
replies.text: It would have been pretty easy to stick a bridge rectifier on the output so you don't lose the "negative" part of the sine wave. Then just stick a smoothing capacitor on the output terminals to reduce the voltage dip and there's a very good chance the flicker would be almost imperceptible.

replies.author: @sofa-lofa4241
replies.text: I think it depends on the type of load, a resistive load like a bulb or heating element should be fine but sensitive electronics could be damaged if there is insufficient input protection

replies.author: @paulmaydaynight9925
replies.text: @sofa-lofa4241 ,today its faster to just put a cheap dc-dc buck/boost on there, set your wanted output then shrink wrap it for outdoor use in this case

replies.author: @tinkmarshino
replies.text: @sofa-lofa4241 ah.. but it is still possible that is what throws me.. Thanks for your information.. I do have a low ac voltage transformers  I might try it with a high repetitive load.. maybe.. thanks Sofa..

replies.author: @tinkmarshino
replies.text: @ChrisHolden yup that would have been easy Chris and smart.. but not the point he was making here.. But it is good info for those that are planning on trying it. As for me I like plating with transformer's to step it back up to 120 (or 220 depending)

author: @NorthernKitty
text: That is fantastic!!  I think the output is actually pretty impressive!!  I have a strip of 60 LED's operating at 12 volts that draws about 4 watts at full power and won't light on less than 1 watt.  Based on that, I'm guessing that your strip needed in excess of 10 watts to get them to light up as bright as they did.  I have a portable 50L refrigerator that averages 8 watts - just a handful of these bike wheels (accounting for low wind) would keep a battery charged so that it could run indefinitely.  Not bad, considering they're dirt cheap to make!!  😊

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: All you need is to add 3 more transformers. This gives you a total of 4 at every 90 degree point on the wheel. Then add in a single full bridge rectifier with smoothing capacitors after connecting the transformers together in parallel (observe winding direction). This will easily put out a constant 10-20 watts in a mild breeze.

author: @whisthpo
text:
And there you go, Free Energy post 'Ze Grayt Rezet'... 
Many applications spring to mind, alternative way to charge your battery bank to run a freezer for fresh meat and PIR driven security lights/prox alarms...
Why don't Motorhomes use windpower to charge the batt bank during foul weather?

replies.author: @sofa-lofa4241
replies.text:
It's do-able but wind turbines tend to create lots of resonance, it can be bad enough attached to a building but I would imagine a motorhome with turbine running wouldn't be the best place to try to sleep,
If you set it up at a short distance then that would probably be a great improvement, but anything not bolted down tends to go missing at campsites

replies.author: @whisthpo
replies.text: @sofa-lofa4241 My bad, I should have been more specific..I had in mind a fixed, ducted wind turbine [as in water turbine ] to be used to charge the battery bank during the journey, and on arrival at destination, could be released to turn into the wind during overcast weather.

replies.author: @sofa-lofa4241
replies.text:
OK, no worries, it would make an interesting project but would increase wind resistance and decrease fuel efficiency while driving, probably better to fit a bigger alternator, 
It would be useful while parked in the daytime but I'm sure the noise would drive you crazy at night!

I have seen a motorhome with a 50 Watt turbine on the roof whilst parked but It was on a hinged mount so it sat between roof boxes while driving/night time/stormy weather, I think its more cost effective to mount a couple of solar panels on then roof to feed a charge controller.... But not half as much fun as a project and expensive too, 

If you can build one similar to this for next to nothing then any power you get is a bonus, but if you are buying new then I would go with panels, 
I think the reason people like solar panels is that they are more consistent, most people who are camping are doing it in reasonable weather (sunny with low wind speeds) but if you are living in it then your priorities may change, maybe a panel/turbine system would be more consistent throughout the year.

replies.author: @whisthpo
replies.text:
@sofa-lofa4241 Great conversation btw, and agree would be good to have both! The secret of solar is storage/capacity...always have more than you are ever likely to use!
The latest panels are much better than 5 yrs ago, have just fitted a 180W [not connected yet] and it was producing 20+V on a really overcast lousy day!

author: @pulesjet
text: Add a Diode and Cap .   Do away with the flickering.   I did the same thing with my bike wheel  wind turbine toy.

replies.author: @ChrisHolden
replies.text: a bridge rectifier would allow you to recover the "negative" part of the sine wave?

replies.author: @pulesjet
replies.text: @ChrisHolden Yes Sir.  Both swings .  Half the Peak to Peak voltage.  Minus the .6v forward drop of the diodes.

replies.author: @pulesjet
replies.text: @ChrisHolden I have a  MB-200 minibike we use to check the mail box here.  LOL  Too lazy to walk the 2 tenths mile and back.  The head light is driven off the tiny  charging coil .    The stupid head light would flicker and dim with engine rpm.   Doing the Bridge and Cap thing ended that mess.  The coil only produces some  4 to 18v depending on RPM driving a  regular 12v  35W bulb.  The cap and diode tamed the mess down most well.   Nominal voltage hits around 14v.  Head light is quite bright.    Slowing down to come to a stop  brightness and dimming  is hardly noticeable.   We only employed a 2000uF cap.

replies.author: @sofa-lofa4241
replies.text: @pulesjet nice hack!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: sure - but I wanted a bare bones test mate
